title: 🎙️ Science, Wonder & the Natural World
description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we look at scientific discovery and the natural world — from ecology and wildlife research to astronomy and deep-sea biology. We're grounding these stories in the landscapes and living systems of the Cariboo: what salmon runs, boreal forest research, or species-at-risk studies tell us about the wild places we live beside. We're after stories that inspire curiosity and wonder about how the world works.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Science, Wonder & the Natural World' — scientific discovery, ecology, natural phenomena, and the curiosity-driven study of living systems. Strong fits include: wildlife biology and species research, ecology studies and biodiversity findings, watershed and salmon research relevant to BC, astronomy and space science, new species discoveries, citizen science projects, natural history stories, wildfire science and forest ecology, climate science findings (not just policy), geology, evolution, and deep-time Earth stories, and any scientific research grounded in BC or Cariboo landscapes and ecosystems. Prefer articles that convey genuine curiosity and discovery — the 'how' and 'why' of the natural world. Score 70–90 for: prehistoric life and palaeontology (ancient oceans, Cretaceous ecology, deep-time species), new species discoveries in ocean or remote environments, deep-sea biology, evolutionary biology findings, natural history stories with a strong sense of wonder, and field research grounded in BC ecosystems. Penalise (score 0–30): consumer health and pharmaceutical research without ecology or natural-world context; pure policy and advocacy news; technology business stories without a science or ecology angle; human interest and viral news without scientific substance; geopolitical and crime news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [CFJC Today Kamloops] Smoke from distant fire darkens Kamloops sky to eerie hue
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Image Credit: CFJC Today / Kent Simmonds By James Peters Wildfire Situation Smoke from distant fire darkens Kamloops sky to eerie hue Jul 7, 2026 | 7:23 PM Share on Facebook Share on Bluesky Share on X Copy Link KAMLOOPS — The sky above Kamloops darkened considerably Tuesday evening (July 7), apparently the result of smoke drifting in from the Brunswick Creek wildfire near Boston Bar. The website FireSmoke.ca showed a forecast of smoke drifting northeast throughout the day, emitted by the 1,800-hectare blaze. The BC Wildfire Service website shows no significant fire activity in the more

items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1964, a Dutch librarian drilled a hole in his own skull to get permanently high
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

According to Bart Huges, the Dutch librarian attended medical school in Amsterdam "but was refused a degree due to his advocacy of marijuana use." In 1964 he published a scroll arguing that trepanation — drilling a hole in the skull — "could be used to enhance brain functionality by balancing the proportion of blood and cerebral spinal fluid." — Read the rest

The post In 1964, a Dutch librarian drilled a hole in his own skull to get permanently high appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1987 Kowloon Walled City packed 33,000 people into six and a half acres
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

By 1987, Kowloon Walled City held an estimated 33,000 residents on 2.6 hectares — "approximately 1.2 million inhabitants per square kilometer (3 million per square mile)," according to Wikipedia, making it one of the most densely populated places on Earth. The 1898 convention that leased the New Territories to Britain excluded the old Chinese fort, leaving an enclave claimed by two governments and governed by neither. — Read the rest

The post In 1987 Kowloon Walled City packed 33,000 people into six and a half acres appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] For 1,000 years people have seen strange lights on the Moon
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

According to the science of transient lunar phenomena, observers have reported brief lights, colors, and hazes on the Moon for at least a thousand years, and astronomers still cannot fully explain them. The British astronomer Patrick Moore coined the term for a 1968 NASA catalog of reported lunar events. — Read the rest

The post For 1,000 years people have seen strange lights on the Moon appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] World's first commercial nuclear-powered payload now in orbit
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

City Labs has launched the world's first commercial satellite carrying a nuclear-powered payload into orbit. Lofted atop a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket, the Betavoltaic Orbital High-Reliability (BOHR) CubeSat is designed to demonstrate using tritium as an energy source.

Continue Reading

Category: Space Systems, Engineering

Tags: Nuclear, Satellite

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Takeaway meals contain more salt than advertised, study finds
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

Almost half of the takeaway meals tested contained more salt than advertised, with some dishes delivering nearly twice the recommended daily limit in a single serving. Surprisingly, classic fish and chips ranked among the lowest-salt options, while pasta, pizza, and curries were often the saltiest.
items.summary: Almost half of the takeaway meals tested contained more salt than advertised, with some dishes delivering nearly twice the recommended daily limit in a single serving. Surprisingly, classic fish and chips ranked among the lowest-salt options, while pasta, pizza, and curries were often the saltiest.
items._excerpt: Almost half of the takeaway meals tested contained more salt than advertised, with some dishes delivering nearly twice the recommended daily limit in a single serving. items.title: [CFJC Today Kamloops] Clearwater ER to close for third consecutive night; fourth in five nights
items.content_html:
🔭 ✏️

(Image Credit: CFJC Today/File Photo) By Victor Kaisar ER Closure Clearwater ER to close for third consecutive night; fourth in five nights Jul 5, 2026 | 6:41 PM Share on Facebook Share on Bluesky Share on X Copy Link CLEARWATER, B.C. — An upcoming emergency room closure at the Dr. Helmcken Memorial Hospital in Clearwater will start 12 hours earlier than initially anticipated. Interior Health says the ER will now close at 7:00 p.m. Sunday (July 5) instead of 7:00 a.m. Monday (July 6) morning. It will remain closed until 8:00 p.m. Monday, meaning a 13 hour closure will now last for 25 hours.

Interior Health says the ER will now close at 7:00 p.m. Sunday (July 5) instead of 7:00 a.m. Monday (July 6) morning. It will remain closed until 8:00 p.m. Monday, meaning a 13 hour closure will now last for 25 hours.
